exemplar.title:
How does the frequency of sound wave (Hz) affect the decibel reduction achieved through active
noise cancellation?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does the frequency of sound wave (Hz) affect the decibel reduction achieved through active

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables include units, consistent significant figures and error bars
Graphing includes a best-fit line, slope and R-value analysis
Use of sample standard deviation demonstrates statistical awareness
weaknesses: Dependent variable mislabeled as ânoise levelâ rather than âdecibel reductionâ
Instrument tolerance and uncertainty propagation are omitted
Some axis labels and captions lack clarity or are misaligned
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is clear in many respects (tables, error bars, regression), albeit not fully precise in labels and captions. Uncertainties are calculated and presented, but without propagation of instrument tolerances or multiple worked examples; data processing is largely correct but conceptually incomplete. Overall performance fits the upper end of the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Research question embedded in a specific, relevant context of ANC headphone applications
Clear, wellâlabelled diagram and stepâbyâstep procedure enabling reasonable reproducibility
Thoughtful identification and justification of key control variables
weaknesses: Minor ambiguities in procedural details (speaker volume, room dimensions) hinder full reproducibility
Inconsistent distance measurements (1 ft vs. 6 in) need resolving
Calibration procedure for noise-measurement app is not specified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a strong grasp of research context and frames a focused question with real-world relevance, meriting the upper band. The apparatus list and procedure allow reproduction with only a few ambiguitiesâaligning with the 5â6 bandâyet minor omissions (volume settings, calibration steps) prevent a perfect score. Methodological considerations are described clearly but not fully explained in exhaustive detail, positioning the work solidly at a 5.

strengths: Conclusion restates observed frequency-dependent trend and ties back to the hypothesis
Qualitative comparison to theory of destructive interference is present
weaknesses: No quantitative interpretation of slope or its uncertainty to support the conclusion
Terminology inconsistency (noise level vs. decibel reduction) undermines logical coherence
Comparison to accepted scientific context remains somewhat general
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is coherent and aligned with the graphed trend, and the student makes an effort to connect results to interference theory. However, it lacks numerical justification, retains conceptual labeling errors, and offers only a general comparison to accepted context. These attributes place it at the upper end of the 3â4 band, meriting a score of 4.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (environmental noise, meter accuracy, microphone placement)
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (sound-proofing, multiple meters, fixed jig)
Provides qualitative assessment of the relative impact of each limitation
weaknesses: Improvements are described rather than fully explained in terms of how they quantitatively reduce uncertainty
Discussion of mitigation strategies could be more detailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes and rates specific methodological limitations and offers practical improvements, linking each weakness to data validity. While the explanations of how improvements reduce uncertainties are brief, the treatment demonstrates thoughtful evaluation at the 3â4 band. The level of insight and relevance of improvements supports a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly phrased and embedded in the concrete context of commercial ANC headphones, with an explicit focus on the relationship between sound-wave frequency and decibel reduction. The introduction links the question to real-world applications (aviation cabins, automotive interiors, hearing aids) and to syllabus concepts (interference, phase difference), providing a specific and appropriate backdrop. Minor wording issues do not detract from the relevance or specificity, hence high-band performance, though not flawless enough to justify the full 6.

Uncertainties are calculated via half-range and presented for each frequency; percentage uncertainties and error bars are cited. Instrument uncertainties, calibration and propagation are omitted, and only one worked example is shown. Thus uncertainties are considered but with significant omissions, fitting the 3-4 descriptor.

The student identifies key controls (distance, room noise, hardware, software) and justifies WHY each must be held constant, showing awareness of relevance. However, sufficiency is weaker: only four test frequencies and no âwithout-ANCâ baseline undermine data adequacy, and explanations of how controls were maintained are brief. This fulfils the descriptor for âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplained,â positioning the work mid-band.

Three specific weaknesses (environmental noise, meter accuracy, microphone placement) are identified, each accompanied by a qualitative rating of impact (low/medium/high) and a brief rationale. The effect on data validity or trend is explicitly discussed, which constitutes an explanation of relative impact. Minor brevity prevents the full 6 but merits high-band placement.

Apparatus, diagram and step-by-step procedure allow another student to repeat the investigation with reasonable fidelity: brand/model consistency, 6-inch microphone distance, five trials, Audacity inversion steps and subtraction of background noise are given. Nevertheless, ambiguities remain (e.g., speaker output volume, calibration of phone meter, exact room dimensions), so reproduction is possible but with a few uncertaintiesâconsistent with the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Means, standard deviations, linear regression and r-value are provided and numerically reasonable. However, the dependent variable is labelled as ânoise levelâ rather than âreductionâ, and the student never processes data to show the ÎdB with vs without ANC. These conceptual inaccuracies constitute notable omissions, so processing is competent but not wholly appropriateâmid-band credit.

For every stated weakness, the student proposes a realistic improvement (sound-proof room, multiple meters, fixed microphone jig). While sensible, these improvements are only described; the explanation of HOW they would mitigate the error is minimal. This aligns with âdescribedâ rather than âexplained,â mid-band 3-4.

Raw-data and processed-data tables are present with headings, units and significant-figure consistency largely intact, and the graph includes a best-fit line and statistical parameters. Precision slips occur (column mis-alignment, missing units in some captions, unclear axis labels on the embedded image), so communication is clear OR precise but not fully bothâmatching the 3-4 band.

The student alludes to textbook explanations of destructive interference and asserts that low-frequency cancellation is easier, but offers only a cursory comparison to accepted scientific understanding (no reference values, no literature data). The treatment is superficial rather than analytical, fitting the lower band.

The conclusion restates the observed linear increase in measured dB with frequency and links this to the hypothesis that ANC is less effective at high frequencies. It is broadly consistent with the graphed trend, though it overlooks the conceptual error in the variable definition and gives no quantitative comparison (e.g., gradient interpretation). Hence the conclusion is âdescribed but not fully consistent,â warranting the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The introduction provides context but could more directly link your personal interest to the specific investigation aims to sharpen focus.
The research question is clearly articulated and embedded within a specific real-world context of ANC headphone applications, demonstrating excellent framing.
The mathematical statement $$A_{\text{total}}=A_1+A_2=0$$ assumes equal amplitudes; add a note addressing phase coherence and realâworld variability.
The hypothesis is specific and testable, predicting clear differences at low and high frequencies.
Subtracting room noise via a phone app requires calibration; include manufacturer tolerance or calibration procedure to justify accuracy.

The background theory describes wave properties but does not yet link explicitly how each concept informs the hypothesis. Add a sentence connecting amplitude, phase and frequency to expected cancellation.
The explanation of interference is clear and well phrased, effectively introducing constructive and destructive cases.
The ANC technology description is detailed but verbose; consider condensing to focus on the mechanism most relevant to your method.
When presenting $$I\propto A^2$$, clarify how intensity relates to decibel levels, e.g. $$L=20\log_{10}(A/A_0)$$ for sound measurement.
The dependent variable is defined as decibel reduction but measured values appear as absolute noise levels. Define and record both baseline and ANCâreduced levels.
Apparatus list is comprehensive; consider specifying speaker volume settings to enable exact replication of sound intensity.
The diagram is clear and wellâlabelled, offering a useful visual overview of the experimental setup.
Setup description conflicts with earlier distance control (1 foot vs. 6 inches). Ensure consistent measurements throughout.
Method steps list subtraction of room noise but omit how values are logged and organized. Clarify table headings and dataâentry format.
Raw data table shows noise levels but lacks separate baseline (ANC off) readings, preventing direct calculation of reduction.
Risk assessment covers general points; consider discussing safe exposure limits and maximum decibel thresholds for ear protection.
Label âRaw Dataâ is present but the actual table of raw vs processed values is not explicitly separated. Add clear headings in the document.
Absolute uncertainty formula $$\Delta x=(\text{max}-\text{min})/2$$ is correctly applied but instrument tolerance was not included in propagation.
The mean and standard deviation calculation uses denominator 4 (Nâ1), which is correct for sample SD; note this in your method to justify statistical choice.
Processed data table still labels values as ânoise levelâ rather than âdecibel reduction,â perpetuating the conceptual inconsistency.
Including error bars and a bestâfit line demonstrates clear communication of processing choices.
The embedded graph image resolution and axis labels are unclear; ensure both axes and units are legible in the final document.
You report slope and R but no significance test or confidence interval; consider adding a pâvalue or CI to strengthen statistical analysis.
Conclusion restates trend but lacks quantitative interpretation of slope and its uncertainty to justify consistency with hypothesis.
The conclusion refers to noise levels rather than reduction; reconcile terminology to maintain logical consistency.
The evaluation section lists weaknesses but could explain how each suggested improvement specifically reduces uncertainty in your measurements.
Error significance and possible improvements table is well structured, showing thoughtful assessment of method limitations.
Possible improvements are described but lack explanation of how, quantitatively, a soundproof room or multiple meters will reduce specific uncertainties.
Extension ideas are realistic but require more detail on implementation (e.g., how to vary and measure background noise quantitatively).
Extension table defines independent and dependent variables clearly, showing sound experimental planning.
For the first extension, specifying instrumentation and calibration steps for measuring background noise will strengthen reproducibility.
Control variables are comprehensively listed for Extension 1, demonstrating appropriate consideration of reproducibility.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:

b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
